join_order.note: 624 qualifying orderkeys probe orders_orderkey_hash; 1:1 match (o_orderkey unique in orders) → 624 order rows
624 o_custkey values probe customer_custkey_hash → 624 customer rows

filter_pushdown.note: Empirically measured (full scan): exactly 624 of 15,000,000 orderkeys qualify. Max sum_qty = 331.0 — threshold is extremely selective. Query Guide estimated ~2400; empirical answer is 624.

logical_plan.sum_qty_reuse: SUM(l_quantity) per orderkey computed once in subquery phase (Step 1). The outer query GROUP BY sum is identical — same table, same join predicate, no additional filter. No second lineitem scan is needed.

data_structures.dim_filter: flat_array<{int32_t orderkey, double sum_qty}>(624) — qualifying orderkeys with precomputed sums from subquery phase
data_structures.fact_join: compact_hash_map pre-sized per thread (262144 slots) for thread-local lineitem aggregation; merged post-scan into single qualifying set
data_structures.aggregation: direct_array (624 result rows — identity grouping since o_orderkey is unique in orders, 1 group per qualifying orderkey)

execution_phases.name: subquery_scan
execution_phases.description: Full parallel scan of lineitem (l_orderkey + l_quantity). 64 threads, morsel-driven, each thread owns a thread-local compact_hash_map<int32_t,double> pre-sized to 262144 slots (~250K expected keys/thread = 15M/64). After all morsels processed, merge 64 thread-local maps: for each key, sum contributions. Filter merged map for sum > 300.0 → exactly 624 qualifying (orderkey, sum_qty) pairs stored in flat_array. These sum_qty values are REUSED for the outer SELECT SUM(l_quantity) — no second lineitem scan needed.

execution_phases.name: orders_index_probe
execution_phases.description: Probe pre-built orders_orderkey_hash (mmap) with 624 qualifying orderkeys. Zero runtime build cost. For each probe: hash32(key) → slot → row_idx. Then random-load o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice at the found row_idx. 624 probes are trivial (single-threaded).

execution_phases.name: customer_index_probe
execution_phases.description: Probe pre-built customer_custkey_hash (mmap) with 624 o_custkey values. Zero runtime build cost. For each probe: hash32(o_custkey) → slot → row_idx. Then load c_name at row_idx*26 from c_name.bin (fixed 26-byte records). 624 probes are trivial.

execution_phases.name: assemble_and_topk
execution_phases.description: Assemble 624 ResultRow tuples combining: c_name, c_custkey (=o_custkey), o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, sum_qty (from Phase 1 flat_array). Then std::partial_sort top 100 by (o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC). 624 rows — trivially fast, no heap needed at this scale.

parallelism.note: Only Phase 1 (lineitem scan) is parallelized. Phases 2-4 operate on ≤624 rows and are single-threaded.

index_usage.purpose: Probe 624 qualifying orderkeys → order row_idxs (zero runtime build cost, mmap)
Probe 624 o_custkey values → customer row_idxs for c_name lookup (zero runtime build cost, mmap)

physical_plan.memory_pattern: streaming_sequential (lineitem scan Phase 1) + random_access_with_prefetch (index probes Phases 2-3)

io_strategy.rationale: No zone maps applicable for lineitem subquery — lineitem is physically sorted by l_shipdate, but subquery has no date predicate. Must full-scan. Orders and customer accessed exclusively via pre-built mmap'd hash indexes (only 624 random lookups each — negligible I/O).
io_strategy.cold_note: On cold start, issue concurrent madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) for l_orderkey.bin and l_quantity.bin before scan. Index files (orders_orderkey_hash: ~462 MB, customer_custkey_hash: ~56 MB) are accessed at only 624 random positions — OS page faults are bounded and tolerable at this scale.

estimated_cost.dominant_phase: subquery_scan (lineitem: 720 MB sequential I/O + 60M-row parallel hash aggregation, 64 threads)
estimated_cost.sampling_note: Empirically measured via full lineitem scan: exactly 624 orderkeys have SUM(l_quantity) > 300.0. Max observed sum = 331.0 (threshold extremely selective). Query Guide estimate of ~2400 was an overestimate. With only 624 result rows, the sort/topk phase is trivially fast (std::partial_sort on 624 elements).
